quick_facts.label: Award and equivalence
quick_facts.value: A PGDM is a postgraduate diploma rather than a university degree. It is often treated as comparable to an MBA in practice, but there is no blanket UGC determination that every PGDM is equivalent to a master’s degree; equivalence for higher education is decided by the receiving university and for employment by the employing organisation.

quick_facts.label: Typical duration
quick_facts.value: The common full-time PGDM format is two years: XLRI identifies its full-time PGDM(BM) and PGDM(HRM) as two-year programmes. Duration is not uniform across all PGDMs; executive and working-professional variants may be shorter or delivered differently.

quick_facts.label: CAT connection
quick_facts.value: CAT is a major route into management programmes and is used by some schools for shortlisting, but it is not universal for PGDM admission. For example, IIM Ahmedabad requires a valid CAT score for its MBA process, while XLRI’s flagship PGDM(BM) and PGDM(HRM) admissions use XAT for Indian candidates.

quick_facts.label: Entry qualification
quick_facts.value: A bachelor’s degree is the usual entry qualification. Minimum marks, work-experience requirements, accepted tests, category relaxations, and final-selection rules are set by each institute and programme; they should be checked in the applicable admission policy.

sections.heading: Eligibility
paragraphs: For a standard two-year PGDM, applicants normally need a recognised bachelor’s degree in any discipline. Institute rules can impose minimum academic scores and category-based relaxations. As a current example of an admissions policy, IIM Ahmedabad’s MBA process requires a bachelor’s degree with at least 50% marks or equivalent CGPA, relaxed to 45% for SC, ST, and PwD candidates; final-year students may be considered subject to completing degree requirements.

A PGDM applicant should not treat CAT’s minimum eligibility as an admission guarantee. Institutes may set separate programme eligibility and shortlisting standards, including entrance-test performance, academic record, work experience, written assessment, group discussion, or interview performance.
bullets: Check that the bachelor’s degree and marks calculation meet the target school’s rules.
Check whether final-year applicants may apply provisionally and the date by which graduation must be completed.
For executive PGDMs, verify work-experience and age requirements separately; these often differ from the regular two-year PGDM.

sections.heading: Duration and study format
paragraphs: A regular PGDM is commonly a two-year postgraduate management programme. XLRI distinguishes its two-year PGDM(BM) and PGDM(HRM) programmes from its executive PGDM, illustrating why applicants should verify the exact duration and delivery mode rather than assume that every programme labelled PGDM has the same format.

Alternative formats exist. For example, XLRI’s 2026–28 blended PGDM programmes are two years across six terms and combine live online teaching with campus immersion, while its executive offerings are designed for experienced professionals and may have a different length.
bullets: Use the institute’s current brochure or admission page to confirm full-time, residential, blended, online, or executive status.
Confirm internship, fieldwork, campus-residency, attendance, and term requirements before applying.

sections.heading: Curriculum
paragraphs: PGDM curricula are set by the awarding institution, so subjects and specialisations vary. A representative official example is XLRI’s management curriculum, which covers marketing, finance, operations, human resources, strategy, and management information systems, with second-year elective concentrations in areas such as finance, marketing, strategy, operations, and MIS.

Applicants should compare the published course structure rather than rely on the PGDM title alone. Useful comparison points include core management coverage, electives, applied projects or fieldwork, faculty, assessment model, industry interface, and the availability of specialisations.
bullets: Core areas often include economics, accounting/finance, marketing, operations, organisational behaviour or HR, strategy, and information systems, but course names and depth differ by school.
Elective choices and specialisations can materially affect the learning path, so verify that the desired area is actually offered to the intended batch.

sections.heading: Admission process
paragraphs: Admission is institute-specific. CAT is relevant because many management schools use it, but a CAT score alone does not create eligibility or admission to every PGDM. For example, IIM Ahmedabad shortlists eligible applicants with valid CAT-2025 scores using its stated process, whereas XLRI’s PGDM(BM) and PGDM(HRM) process for Indian candidates uses XAT and includes later selection stages.

Selection may be multi-factor. XLRI states that, in addition to test performance, its process considers personal interviews and may consider relevant work experience, academic background, and extracurricular activities. Candidates should use the policy for the exact intake year because test windows, application stages, and selection weights can change.
bullets: Identify each target programme’s accepted examination(s): CAT, XAT, GMAT, GRE, or another stated test.
Apply separately to the school or programme where required; taking an entrance test does not necessarily complete a programme application.
Treat cut-offs, application dates, interview dates, and selection weights as intake-year-specific information.

sections.heading: Costs and financial planning
paragraphs: Fees vary substantially by school, programme format, campus, and intake year. As a dated 2026 example, XLRI lists a fee of ₹15.25 lakh per annum for its PGDM(BM)/PGDM(HRM) admissions information and states that mess and hostel expenses are extra; it also reserves the right to revise the fee. This is an institution-specific illustration, not a nationwide PGDM fee benchmark.

Before accepting an offer, obtain the current fee schedule and separate tuition from application fees, hostel and mess charges, deposits, learning materials, travel, insurance, and other personal costs. Also check scholarship, fee-waiver, loan, and refund terms in the current official documents.
bullets: Do not compare programmes using tuition alone; include compulsory living and programme charges.
Use only the current intake’s official fee notice when budgeting, since published fees and policies can change.

sections.heading: What a PGDM can lead to
paragraphs: A PGDM can support progression into management employment or further study, but recognition requirements must be checked for the specific purpose. UGC states that it does not determine PGDM-to-master’s equivalence; the receiving university decides equivalence for higher-education purposes and the employer decides it for employment purposes. Therefore, candidates should obtain confirmation from the target university, recruiter, public employer, or licensing body before relying on a PGDM for a particular eligibility requirement.

Some doctoral programmes explicitly accept qualifying two-year full-time PGDMs. For example, XLRI lists a route to its Fellow Programme in Management for candidates holding a two-year full-time PGDM/MBA or equivalent from specified institutions and meeting its stated academic threshold. This is an example of a programme-specific route, not a universal doctoral-admission rule.
bullets: For jobs, verify the employer’s wording on accepted qualifications where a vacancy specifies an MBA or master’s degree.
For doctoral or other higher-study applications, ask the receiving institution whether the particular PGDM and awarding school meet its eligibility rules.
Retain official transcripts, diploma documents, approval/accreditation evidence where applicable, and the institute’s programme details.
